socialTemplates.title: Launch Day: The Story That Started Everything
socialTemplates.body:
December 7, 2010.
I was 22 years old. Playing basketball at East Stroudsburg University. And then — nothing.
Sudden Cardiac Arrest. My heart stopped.
I'm here today because of a machine and the courage of three people who knew what to do.
Athletic trainers Wendy Dietrich and Colleen Shotwell. EMT Douglas Shaffer.
And because a 15-year-old named Greg Moyer — who died on a court just like that one in 2000 —
had parents who turned their grief into a defibrillator fund that placed AEDs in schools and gyms
across Monroe County. One of those AEDs restarted my heart.
Greg didn't get a second chance. I did.
I built Latimore Life & Legacy LLC because of that second chance.
Not to sell insurance. To extend the chain of preparation that saved my life —
now protecting the financial lives and legacies of families right here in Schuylkill County.
Today is Day 1. And #TheBeatGoesOn.
socialTemplates.cta: 🔗 www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Start the conversation

socialTemplates.title: What Is Life Insurance, Really? (Not What You Were Taught)
socialTemplates.body:
Most people think life insurance is a product you buy when you're old.
Something you get around to eventually.
Here's what it actually is:
Life insurance is a promise.
A promise that if you're not here tomorrow, your family doesn't lose the house.
Your kids don't grow up wondering how the bills got paid.
Your spouse doesn't have to start over from zero.
And with modern policies — it's also a promise that pays out WHILE YOU'RE ALIVE.
A heart attack. A stroke. A cancer diagnosis.
Living Benefits let you access your policy when life happens — not just when it ends.
That's not fear. That's preparation.
And preparation is what I do.
socialTemplates.cta: 🔗 www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Book a free review

socialTemplates.title: The $898 Nobody Told You About
socialTemplates.body:
67% of American parents don't have a legal will.
Without one, the state decides who gets your money, your house — and who raises your kids.
A lawyer charges $1,500–$2,500 to draft the basics.
LegalZoom runs $249+. Trust & Will, $499+.
Here's what Latimore Life & Legacy clients get:
• Legal Will

All five. $0. Included with every eligible policy.

socialTemplates.title: AED Awareness Month: The Machine That Saved My Life
socialTemplates.body:
May is AED Awareness Month.
I don't talk about this because I have to. I talk about it because I have to.
An Automated External Defibrillator is a portable device that delivers a shock to the heart
when it goes into a dangerous rhythm. In cardiac arrest — when the heart stops —
an AED combined with CPR can double or triple survival rates.
In 2000, Greg Moyer was 15 years old when his heart stopped during a basketball game.
There was no AED. He didn't survive.
His parents launched a fund that placed AEDs in schools and gyms across Monroe County.
In 2010, my heart stopped during a basketball game at ESU.
There was an AED — placed by the Moyer Fund.
I survived.
This month, I'm asking you to do one thing:
Find out where your nearest AED is. Right now. In your workplace. In your child's school.
In your gym. In your church.
Preparation saves lives. Greg's family taught me that.
#TheBeatGoesOn
socialTemplates.cta: 🔗 www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Learn more about our mission

socialTemplates.title: What Is a Living Benefit? The Option Your Policy Might Already Have
socialTemplates.body:
If someone asked you what your life insurance policy covers — what would you say?
Most people say: 'It pays my family when I die.'
That's true. But modern policies — like the ones available through Latimore Life & Legacy —
may also pay YOU while you're still alive.
Here's how it works:
A Living Benefit (also called an Accelerated Death Benefit) allows you to access
a portion of your death benefit if you're diagnosed with a qualifying critical, chronic,
or terminal illness. A heart attack. A stroke. A cancer diagnosis.
In Schuylkill County, where heart disease mortality is nearly double the state average,
this isn't a footnote. It's the whole reason to have a permanent policy.
Does your current policy have this? Do you know?
Let's find out together.

socialTemplates.title: The Cost Myth: What Life Insurance Actually Costs Per Day
socialTemplates.body:
I hear it constantly: 'Life insurance is too expensive.'
So let's talk real numbers.
A healthy 35-year-old can often get $500,000 in term life coverage for around $20–35 per month.
That's about $1 per day. Less than a cup of coffee.
For a healthy 40-year-old: $500K can run $30–55/month. Still less than most streaming subscriptions.
What does that $1/day buy?
• Your mortgage gets paid if you're gone
• Your kids' college stays funded
• Your spouse doesn't have to start over from zero
• Your family gets breathing room instead of crisis
The myth is expensive. The protection is not.
Check your actual rate. Takes 2 minutes. Zero commitment.

socialTemplates.title: Back to School: 5 Protection Questions for Parents
socialTemplates.body:
Back to school season. The lists are long.
Backpacks. Supplies. Sports physicals. School clothes.
Here's one more checklist — this one actually matters long-term.
1. If you weren't here to pick them up from school — who would?
   Is that person legally designated in a will?
2. Who pays the mortgage or rent if you're gone?
3. Does your work life insurance policy follow you if you change jobs?
   (Spoiler: it doesn't.)
4. Does your policy have a Living Benefit?
   What happens if you survive a serious illness — can you access your policy while you're alive?
5. When did you last review your life insurance?
   If you have kids at home — your old policy may not reflect your real needs.
This doesn't have to be complicated. One conversation, 30 minutes.
Completely free. No pressure.

socialTemplates.title: Life Insurance Awareness Month: 4 Things Most People Get Wrong
socialTemplates.body:
September is Life Insurance Awareness Month.
So let's clear up the four biggest myths I hear in Schuylkill County.
MYTH 1: 'I'm too young to need life insurance.'
TRUTH: The younger and healthier you are, the lower the cost — permanently.
Waiting doesn't save money. It costs money.
MYTH 2: 'My work coverage is enough.'
TRUTH: Work coverage is usually 1–2x your salary and disappears when the job does.
It doesn't travel. It doesn't cover Living Benefits. It's not sized for your family.
MYTH 3: 'Life insurance is for when you die.'
TRUTH: Modern permanent policies can pay out while you're still alive.
A heart attack. A stroke. A cancer diagnosis. Living Benefits make this possible.
MYTH 4: 'It's too complicated.'
TRUTH: With Ethos, you can get covered in about 10 minutes. No doctor. No blood test.
And I'm here to make the rest of it simple.
Which myth did you believe? Drop it in the comments.

socialTemplates.title: Financial Literacy Month: 3 Conversations Every Family Needs to Have
socialTemplates.body:
October is Financial Literacy Month.
And I want to give you something more useful than a generic post about budgets.
Here are the three financial conversations most families avoid —
and why avoiding them is the most expensive thing you can do.
CONVERSATION 1: What happens if one of us dies?
Not a fun question. But if you have a mortgage, kids, or any shared debt —
the answer to this question determines whether your family survives financially.
Have this conversation. Write it down. Make a plan.
CONVERSATION 2: What happens if one of us gets seriously ill?
This is the one nobody talks about. A stroke. A cancer diagnosis. A heart attack.
In Schuylkill County, this isn't hypothetical. 178 per 100,000 — nearly double the state average.
Does your insurance pay out while you're still alive? It should.
CONVERSATION 3: What happens to our stuff when we both die?
Who raises the kids? Who gets the house? Who can access the accounts?
Without a will, the state decides. With a will — you decide.
These conversations are hard. Having them is an act of love.
socialTemplates.cta: Link: www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Book a free family financial review

socialTemplates.title: Tax Season Tip: Why Your Life Insurance Matters to Your CPA
socialTemplates.body:
Tax season is here. Before you hand your CPA a folder —
here are two things about life insurance that belong in the conversation.
1. DEATH BENEFITS ARE GENERALLY INCOME-TAX FREE.
If your beneficiary receives a death benefit — in most cases, it's not taxable income.
That's a significant planning advantage for high-income families or business owners.
2. CASH VALUE IN PERMANENT POLICIES GROWS TAX-DEFERRED.
If you have a permanent life insurance policy with cash value —
that growth is not taxed until you access it.
Some business owners use this as a supplemental retirement vehicle.
NOT TAX ADVICE: Always work with a qualified CPA or tax professional
for decisions specific to your situation.
But if your CPA doesn't know what you have in place — this is a good time to tell them.
And if you don't have anything in place — let's fix that.
socialTemplates.cta: Link: www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Schedule a free review

socialTemplates.title: American Heart Month: The Day My Heart Stopped — and What I Built Because of It
socialTemplates.body:
February is American Heart Month.
And I want to tell you the full story — not the short version.
December 7, 2010. East Stroudsburg University. Koehler Fieldhouse.
I was 22 years old. Running up and down the court in a pickup basketball game.
And then — nothing. Darkness. My heart stopped.
Sudden Cardiac Arrest.
Three people saved my life that day:
Athletic trainer Wendy Dietrich. Athletic trainer Colleen Shotwell. EMT Douglas Shaffer.
Using an AED placed in that building by the Gregory W. Moyer Defibrillator Fund.
Greg Moyer was 15 years old when he died on a basketball court in 2000.
No AED. No second chance.
His parents turned their grief into a fund. That fund put an AED in the building where I collapsed.
I survived because a grieving family chose to prepare.
That is #TheBeatGoesOn.
That is why Latimore Life & Legacy exists.
That is why I show up every day to protect families in this county.
This February — share this story.
You might save a life just by making someone aware.
socialTemplates.cta: Link: www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Learn more about our mission

socialTemplates.title: How to Use an AED — 4 Steps That Could Save Your Life
socialTemplates.body:
February is American Heart Month.
And I want to give you something practical.
If someone collapsed in front of you right now —
could you use an AED?
Yes. Here's how. Four steps.
STEP 1: Call 911 immediately. Do not wait.
STEP 2: Turn on the AED. It will talk you through everything.
   No medical training required. It's designed for bystanders.
STEP 3: Apply the pads as shown in the diagram.
   One under the right collarbone. One on the left side of the chest.
STEP 4: Stand clear. Let the AED analyze the rhythm.
   If it recommends a shock — make sure no one is touching the patient. Press the button.
   Then immediately resume CPR.
CPR + AED can double or triple cardiac arrest survival rates.
Share this post. Save it. Show it to someone who might need it.
Preparation saves lives. I know — because it saved mine.
#TheBeatGoesOn
socialTemplates.cta: Link: www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Find your nearest AED

socialTemplates.title: Spring Forward: Time to Renew Your Protection
socialTemplates.body:
Daylight Saving. Spring Forward.
We add an hour to the day.
We open the windows.
We remember what sunlight feels like.
And every year, I remind myself:
Spring is the right time to look at the things you've been putting off.
When did you last review your life insurance?
Does your coverage still match your life?
Have you had a baby, bought a house, changed jobs, or gotten married since your last review?
If any of that is true — your coverage might be outdated.
And outdated coverage is just an expensive false comfort.
Spring forward. Review your protection.
Free. 30 minutes. No pressure.
socialTemplates.cta: Link: www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Spring coverage review

socialTemplates.title: Year 1 Done. Here's What We Learned. Here's What Comes Next.
socialTemplates.body:
Year 1 of Latimore Life & Legacy LLC: Complete.
Here's what I learned:
Community wins every time.
The conversations that mattered most didn't start with a pitch.
They started at a football game. At a workshop. At a Chamber meeting.
At a coffee chat where nobody was selling anything.
Story is infrastructure.
The cardiac arrest story isn't marketing — it's the foundation.
Every policy placed this year was placed by someone who trusted a survivor.
Trust is built on authenticity. Authenticity is built on story.
Preparation is love.
Every family that sat down with me this year was an act of love.
Not fear. Not obligation. Love — for the people who matter most.
Year 2 starts with more of all three.
#TheBeatGoesOn. Always.
socialTemplates.cta: Link: www.latimorelifelegacy.com — Join Year 2

socialTemplates.title: To Every Family We Protected in Year 1: Thank You
socialTemplates.body:
This is the last post of Year 1.
To every family who sat across from me.
Who answered the hard questions.
Who said yes to a plan.
Who referred a neighbor, a sibling, a colleague.
Who scanned a QR code at a football game and decided to find out more.
Thank you.
You are the reason #TheBeatGoesOn.
Not as a hashtag — as a truth.
Greg Moyer's family prepared when they didn't have to.
That preparation saved my life.
You let me extend that chain to your family.
And I don't take that lightly — not for a single conversation.
Year 2 starts Monday.
Same mission. Deeper roots. Bigger reach.
See you there.

emails.title: APRIL EMAIL NEWSLETTER — Issue #1
subjectOptions: A: The story behind Latimore Life & Legacy — and why it matters to your family
B: I survived a cardiac arrest. Here's what I built because of it.
C: A new kind of protection is here in Schuylkill County
emails.body:
Hi [First Name],
My name is Jackson Latimore. I'm a Schuylkill County native, a cardiac arrest survivor,
and the founder of Latimore Life & Legacy LLC.
On December 7, 2010, my heart stopped during a pickup basketball game at East Stroudsburg University.
I survived because an AED placed by the Gregory W. Moyer Defibrillator Fund was in that building.
Greg Moyer was 15 when he died on a court just like that one — and his family turned that loss into preparation.
That preparation saved my life.
I started Latimore Life & Legacy because of that second chance.
Not to sell you a policy. To extend that same chain of preparation to the families of this community.
Here's what we do:
We help families in Schuylkill, Luzerne, and Northumberland counties protect their income, their homes,
and their legacies — with life insurance that doesn't just pay when you die,
but may pay when you survive a serious illness too.
We also include something most people don't expect: a free estate planning package with every eligible policy.
Legal will. Living trust. Power of attorney. Healthcare directive. $898 value. Zero added cost.
This month, I'm inviting 10 families to schedule a completely free, no-pressure Protection Review.
30 minutes. Virtual or in-person. No obligation.
If you'd like to be one of them — hit reply or book directly at the link below.
Protecting Today. Securing Tomorrow.
Jackson M. Latimore Sr.
Founder & CEO — Latimore Life & Legacy LLC
PA DOI #1268820 | (856) 895-1457
jackson1989@latimorelegacy.com | latimorelifelegacy.com
#TheBeatGoesOn

Hi [First Name],
May is AED Awareness Month.
I don't share my cardiac arrest story to make you uncomfortable.
I share it because I was 22 years old, healthy, and active — and it happened anyway.
And because the only thing that stood between me and the outcome Greg Moyer's family endured
was a machine that someone had the foresight to place in that building.
This month, I'm doing two things:
1. I'm running an AED Awareness social media campaign throughout May.
   Follow along at Facebook.com/LatimoreLifeLegacy
2. I'm hosting a FREE community AED awareness workshop in June.
   We'll cover what an AED is, how to use one, and where they should be in our community.
   Space is limited — reserve your spot at the link below.
And as always — if you'd like a free, no-pressure Protection Review for your family,
I'm booking appointments now.
Protecting Today. Securing Tomorrow.
Jackson M. Latimore Sr.
Founder & CEO — Latimore Life & Legacy LLC
(856) 895-1457 | jackson1989@latimorelegacy.com

emails.title: SEPTEMBER EMAIL NEWSLETTER — LIAM + Homecoming Edition
subjectOptions: A: It's Life Insurance Awareness Month — and I have something honest to tell you
B: Homecoming is this Friday. We'll be at the booth. Will you?
C: 4 things most people get wrong about life insurance (and the truth about each)
emails.body:
Hi [First Name],
September is Life Insurance Awareness Month. And I want to use this month
to be genuinely useful to you — not to pitch you, but to educate you.
Here are the four things I hear most often from families in our county —
and what's actually true about each of them:
1. 'My work coverage is enough.'
   It's not — it disappears when the job does, and it's not sized for your family's real needs.
2. 'Life insurance is too expensive.'
   For a healthy person your age, it may cost less per day than a coffee. Check your actual rate.
3. 'I'm too young / too healthy to worry about it.'
   Young and healthy is exactly when it's cheapest. Waiting costs real money.
4. 'Life insurance only matters when you die.'
   With Living Benefits, your policy may pay out during a serious illness — while you're alive.
   In Schuylkill County, with our heart disease rates, this is not a footnote. It's the core offer.
This weekend is PAHS Homecoming. We'll be at the booth — biggest setup of the season.
Come find us and ask me anything. No sales pitch. Just honest answers.
And if you can't make the game — book a free review online anytime.
Jackson M. Latimore Sr.
Founder & CEO — Latimore Life & Legacy LLC
(856) 895-1457 | jackson1989@latimorelegacy.com

Every January, people make resolutions about their bodies, their habits, their finances.
Most of those resolutions fade by February.
But there's one financial resolution that keeps working whether you think about it or not:
Life insurance.
Not because it's exciting. Because it's the floor.
The thing that holds everything else up if something goes wrong.
This month, I'm inviting you to spend 30 minutes reviewing your family's protection.
Where are you covered? Where are the gaps? What would it take to close them?
And in February — I'll be sharing something very personal.

emails.title: FEBRUARY EMAIL NEWSLETTER — Heart Month Special Edition
subjectOptions: A: December 7, 2010 — the day my heart stopped
B: American Heart Month: The story I tell every February
C: What I built because I got a second chance
emails.body:
Hi [First Name],
I want to tell you the whole story this month.
December 7, 2010. East Stroudsburg University.
I was 22 years old. Playing basketball. And my heart stopped.
Sudden Cardiac Arrest.
I survived because athletic trainers Wendy Dietrich and Colleen Shotwell,
and EMT Douglas Shaffer, used an AED that had been placed in Koehler Fieldhouse
by the Gregory W. Moyer Defibrillator Fund.
Greg Moyer was 15 when he died on a basketball court in 2000. No AED was available.
His parents channeled their grief into a fund that saved my life ten years later.
That's why I started Latimore Life & Legacy.
Not to sell insurance. To honor that chain of preparation.
This February, I'm asking you to do two things:
1. Find out where your nearest AED is. Right now.
   At your workplace. Your child's school. Your gym. Your church.
2. Make sure your family is financially prepared — not just for death, but for survival.
   A serious illness. A cardiac event. A stroke. Things that happen while you're still here.
   Living Benefits. Estate planning. A will. These things matter.
If you want help with the second one — I'm here. Free. No pressure.
This month, more than any other — #TheBeatGoesOn means something.
Jackson M. Latimore Sr.
Founder & CEO — Latimore Life & Legacy LLC
(856) 895-1457 | jackson1989@latimorelegacy.com

And a continued commitment to showing up — not just marketing, but actually being here.
If you've been thinking about a coverage review and haven't booked yet — now is a great time.
Spring is when people reassess. And this spring, I want to help as many families as possible
start the new season with a plan.
Book your free spring coverage review at the link below.
30 minutes. Honest. Free.
Thank you for Year 1.
Here's to Year 2.
Jackson M. Latimore Sr.
Founder & CEO — Latimore Life & Legacy LLC
(856) 895-1457 | jackson1989@latimorelegacy.com
#TheBeatGoesOn

scripts.label: APRIL BRAND VIDEO SCRIPT
scripts.body: Record Week 3. Film at a meaningful location — either indoors at a desk (professional) or outdoors in Pottsville (community feel). Keep to 90 seconds. Captions are mandatory.

scripts.label: BRAND INTRODUCTION VIDEO SCRIPT — 90 SECONDS:
scripts.body:
[OPEN on Jackson, looking directly into camera. Calm, confident.]
'On December 7, 2010, I was 22 years old, playing basketball — and my heart stopped.'
[Brief pause.]
I survived because someone prepared before I needed them to.
A defibrillator fund started by a family who lost their son — Greg Moyer — on a court just like that one.
His family's preparation gave me a second chance.
[Shift — Jackson's expression firms up.]
'I built Latimore Life & Legacy because of that second chance.
I'm here to help families in Schuylkill County prepare — not out of fear, but out of love.'
[Final beat — direct and warm.]
'Life insurance that pays while you're still alive. A free legal will with every eligible policy.
A community advisor who actually shows up. That's what we do.'
[Text on screen: latimorelifelegacy.com | #TheBeatGoesOn]
'Protecting Today. Securing Tomorrow.'
APRIL COMMUNITY COFFEE CHAT — Event Script
Host Week 4. Target 10+ attendees. Venue: local coffee shop, library community room, or Chamber space. Format: 45 minutes total — 20 min presentation, 25 min open Q&A.
COMMUNITY COFFEE CHAT — OPENING REMARKS (5 minutes):
'Thank you all for being here. I'm Jackson Latimore — some of you know me, some of you are meeting me
for the first time. Either way, I'm glad you came.
I want to start with a story, not a sales pitch.
[Tell the cardiac arrest story — 3 minutes, conversational, from memory — Greg Moyer, ESU, December 2010,
the AED, the three people who saved his life.]
The reason I'm telling you that story is simple:
I believe preparation is an act of love. And that's what Latimore Life & Legacy is built on.
Tonight I'm not here to sell anything. I'm here to answer questions.
If you leave tonight with one thing — one clarity about your own family's financial protection —
then this was worth your time.'
DISCUSSION TOPICS (choose 2–3 based on audience):
• 'What happens to my family financially if I die before I retire?'
• 'What is a Living Benefit and why does it matter in Schuylkill County?'
• 'Do I really need a will? And how much should it cost?'
• 'What's the difference between term and whole life insurance?'
CLOSE:
'Before you leave — I have a simple offer. A free 30-minute Protection Review.
I will look at your family's specific situation and tell you honestly what you have, what you need,
and what it would cost to close any gaps. No pressure. No obligation.'
[Hand out cards with QR code linking to card.latimorelifelegacy.com/pahs]

scripts.label: JUNE AED COMMUNITY WORKSHOP — Full Script
AED COMMUNITY WORKSHOP SCRIPT — 45 MINUTES
scripts.body:
OPENING (5 minutes):
'Thank you for being here tonight. My name is Jackson Latimore.
I want to start by telling you why this workshop exists.
[Tell the cardiac arrest story: Greg Moyer, ESU, December 2010, the AED, survival]
I'm alive because of preparation. Tonight, we're going to talk about how you can
be that preparation for someone in your life.'
SECTION 1: WHAT IS CARDIAC ARREST? (8 minutes)
• Cardiac arrest vs. heart attack — the critical difference (heart attack: blockage; cardiac arrest: electrical failure)
• 'Sudden cardiac arrest kills approximately 350,000 Americans per year outside a hospital'
• The 4-minute window: brain damage begins in 4 minutes; death likely in 8–10 without intervention
• CPR + AED together can double or triple survival rates
SECTION 2: HOW TO USE AN AED (12 minutes)
• AEDs are designed for layperson use — no medical training required
• Turn it on — it will talk you through every step
• Apply pads (diagram shown) — one under right collarbone, one under left arm
• AED analyzes rhythm — do not touch the patient
• If shock advised: stand clear, press button
• Resume CPR immediately after shock
[Practice with demo unit if available]
SECTION 3: WHERE ARE YOUR AEDs? (10 minutes)
• Review local AED locations in Schuylkill County schools, gyms, public buildings
• How to get an AED for your business or organization
• The Greg Moyer Defibrillator Fund — and how to support it
CLOSE (10 minutes Q&A + offer):
'Before we close — I want to make you an offer.
Financial preparation and physical preparation are the same idea.
If you don't have a life insurance plan, or if you're not sure what you have,
I'd like to offer you a free 30-minute review — no pressure, no obligation.
Take a card on your way out. The QR code books directly into my calendar.'
[Distribute cards with card.latimorelifelegacy.com/pahs QR code]

scripts.label: AUGUST GAME DAY BOOTH — Complete Activation Script
GAME DAY BOOTH SCRIPT — UNIVERSAL FLOW (use at every home game)
scripts.body:
SETUP: Arrive 2.5 hours before kickoff. Tent up, banner visible, table set, programs stacked.
QR code display facing traffic. Giveaways accessible. Business cards in hand.
OPENING LINE (when someone approaches within 5 feet):
'Hey! Great night for football — do you have a player on the team?'
OR: 'Can I hand you one of these? It's the official game program — rosters, schedule, everything. Free.'
OR (for moving crowd): 'Scan this real quick — free life insurance quote, 2 minutes on your phone.'
BRIDGE TO BRAND (15–20 seconds):
'We're the Official Protection Partner of the Crimson Tide this year — Latimore Life & Legacy.
I'm Jackson. We help families in Schuylkill County protect their income, their homes, and their legacy.
Life insurance that can actually pay out while you're still alive — not just when you're gone.'
THE TWO-PART OFFER (choose based on audience read):
OPTION A — Speed: 'We use Ethos — 100% online, no doctor, no blood test.
   Most people get covered in about 10 minutes from their phone. Scan this.'
OPTION B — Value: 'Every policy we write includes a free legal will, living trust, and power of attorney.
   $898 value. Zero extra cost. Most families put this off for years. We make it a bonus.'
OPTION C — Living Benefits: 'In a county where heart disease is nearly double the state average —
   your life insurance should pay out while you're alive if something happens. Does yours?'
DIAGNOSTIC (identify where they are):
'Quick question — have you reviewed your life insurance in the last year or two?'
YES: 'Great. Does your policy have a Living Benefit rider?'
NO: 'That's actually really common. The good news is it takes about 10 minutes online.'
LEAD CAPTURE (close or book):
'What's the best number to text you? I'll send the link right now.'
OR: 'Scan this QR code — goes straight to our page. Free quote, 2 minutes.'
OR: 'Let me grab your info and I'll follow up Thursday with a couple of options.'
EXIT: 'Enjoy the game. Go Tide!'
[Log every interaction in CRM within 15 minutes. Follow up within 48 hours. No exceptions.]

scripts.label: OCTOBER FINANCIAL LITERACY WORKSHOP #1 — Full Script
WORKSHOP SCRIPT: Income Protection — What Happens to Your Family If You Can't Work?
scripts.body:
45 minutes | Community center / library / church setting | No product sales on-site
OPENING (5 minutes):
My name is Jackson Latimore. I'm a cardiac arrest survivor and the founder of Latimore Life & Legacy.
Tonight is not a sales presentation. Tonight is a conversation about something most families
have never sat down to think through together.
If your income stopped tomorrow — what happens?
SECTION 1: THE INCOME DEPENDENCY PROBLEM (10 minutes)
Ask the room: How many of you have a monthly mortgage or rent payment? [Hands go up]
How many of you could keep making that payment for 12 months if you lost your income? [Hands go down]
This is the income dependency problem. Most families have 30–90 days of runway.
After that — the house, the car, the lifestyle — it all becomes at risk.
SECTION 2: THREE INCOME DISRUPTION RISKS (12 minutes)
1. Death — the income stops permanently. The family absorbs the loss.
2. Disability — the income stops temporarily or permanently. The bills don't stop.
3. Critical illness — income may stop while you fight to survive.
   [Note: In Schuylkill County, heart disease mortality is nearly 2x the state average.
   This is not an abstraction for this audience.]
SECTION 3: WHAT INCOME PROTECTION LOOKS LIKE (10 minutes)
Term life insurance — income replacement at the largest scale for the lowest cost.
Permanent life insurance with Living Benefits — protection that pays even if you survive.
Annuities — guaranteed income that cannot be outlived, regardless of market conditions.
CLOSE + Q&A (8 minutes):
Tonight I want to leave you with one action item: find out what you have.
Pull out your policy. Read the coverage amount. Read the riders.
If you don't have a policy — or if you're not sure what yours covers —
I'm offering a free 30-minute review. No commitment. No sales pitch.
[Distribute cards with QR code — card.latimorelifelegacy.com/pahs]

scripts.label: FEBRUARY CARDIAC ARREST AWARENESS WORKSHOP — Full Script
FEBRUARY CARDIAC ARREST AWARENESS WORKSHOP SCRIPT — 60 MINUTES
scripts.body:
This is the signature community event of the year. Invite local health organizations.
Request media coverage. This is a public health event, not a sales event.
OPENING (8 minutes):
My name is Jackson Latimore.
On December 7, 2010, I was 22 years old and my heart stopped on a basketball court.
I'm alive because of three people and one machine.
This workshop exists because I believe everyone in this room could be those three people
for someone in this community.
SECTION 1: THE CARDIAC ARREST EMERGENCY (15 minutes)
350,000 Americans die from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest every year.
Fewer than 10% survive without bystander intervention.
Brain damage begins in 4 minutes. The window is small.
CPR + AED response in the first few minutes can dramatically increase survival odds.
In Schuylkill County — with our elevated heart disease rates — this is not an abstraction.
SECTION 2: CPR FUNDAMENTALS (15 minutes)
Hands-only CPR is effective and does not require training.
Call 911 first. Then begin chest compressions — hard and fast, center of chest.
100–120 compressions per minute. Think: the beat of 'Stayin Alive' by the Bee Gees.
[Practice session if hands-on training certified facilitator available]
SECTION 3: AED DEMONSTRATION (12 minutes)
[Use demo AED unit] Walk through the four steps: Turn on. Apply pads. Stand clear. Shock.
Show where AEDs are located in your local community.
Discuss how to get an AED for your business or organization.
SECTION 4: THE FINANCIAL SIDE OF SURVIVING (5 minutes)
Surviving cardiac arrest is step one. Surviving the financial aftermath is step two.
A critical illness rider on a life insurance policy can pay out during recovery.
Tonight I'm not selling anything. But I want you to know this option exists.
If you'd like to explore it — I have cards at the door with a free review link.
CLOSE + Q&A (5 minutes):
Thank the audience. Remind them of two actions: find their nearest AED, share tonight's information.
[Distribute cards with card.latimorelifelegacy.com/pahs QR code]
